---
bibliography: [refs.bib]
---

# Introduction

Universities and other higher education institutions are central to producing and disseminating knowledge, contributing to human capital development. Instructors' choices -- how to teach, which materials to assign, and whether to collaborate with colleagues -- shape students' educational experience, influencing their learning outcomes and career trajectories substantially [@bakkenes2010teacher;@vembye2024effects;@han2023interdisciplinary]. These choices are driven by individual preferences and institutional constraints, which may not align with students' learning needs. For instance, instructors may prefer working alone or with colleagues from similar backgrounds, which can limit course diversity and interdisciplinarity, reinforce gender stereotypes in specific subjects [@ellemers2018gender;@lockwood2006someone;@canaan2023impact]. Collaboration can also shape instructors' professional development by exposing them to different teaching methodologies [@buckingham2021learning]. Yet, despite their significance, the factors that drive these teaching choices remain poorly understood, primarily due to a lack of data.

This study addresses this gap by analysing a large dataset of English course syllabi from 1990 to 2019 across fifteen countries and from 2019 to 2023 for thirty-three European countries, which has previously supported research on school curricula [@han2023interdisciplinary], teaching metrics [@nowogrodzki2016mining], the impacts of AI [@gao2023quantifying], and the education-innovation gap [@biasi2022education]. The dataset enables a systematic, quantitative analysis of long-term trends. Each syllabus includes the instructor's gender, institutional affiliation, course field, assigned readings, and a course description. This information allows us to examine gender-based team formation, revealing potential preferences and institutional constraints in teaching collaborations. Furthermore, we analyse how team composition, particularly gender and size, relates to teaching practices, such as interdisciplinarity and reading assignments, while controlling for institutional factors, field, academic year, and other relevant variables.

Our work establishes links with four strands of literature. Research shows that gender and organisational constraints shape academic team formation [e.g., @guimera2005team; @wuchty2007increasing; @milojevic2014principles; @yang2022gender], but it is unclear whether the same applies to teaching teams. Second, instructor gender can influence the representation of women scholars in citations and course readings [e.g., @dion2018gendered], but it is unclear whether mixed-gender teams may offset this effect. Third, mixed-gender teams have been linked to greater interdisciplinarity in research [e.g., @pinheiro2022women], though evidence in teaching contexts is limited. Finally, gender-diverse teams tend to adopt more novel or unconventional approaches [e.g., @yang2022gender], a pattern we explore in the context of pedagogical innovation. 

Building on this literature, this study examines how gender and institutional constraints shape the formation of teaching teams, whether instructor gender affects the inclusion of female-authored readings in course syllabi, and whether mixed-gender teams promote greater interdisciplinarity in course content. We also investigate whether gender-diverse teaching teams are more likely to adopt novel or unconventional readings and teaching methods ("teaching novelty"), including materials that are recent ("recency") or at the frontier of research ("scientific novelty"). Previous research shows that these aspects shape students' learning outcomes and career trajectories [@bakkenes2010teacher; @vembye2024effects; @han2023interdisciplinary], and they also influence instructors' professional development and promotion within their institutions [@buckingham2021learning].

We complement the above analysis with an exploration of the formation of teaching teams pre- and post- COVID-19 in Europe. The COVID-19 pandemic disrupted academic work in many EU countries, reducing productivity, especially for women and caregivers [e.g., @myers2020unequal], and reshaping collaboration networks. Also, virtual conferences increased participation but limited informal interactions vital for teaching collaborations. The shift to online and hybrid teaching may also have affected team formation across gender and disciplines. Using COVID-19 as a quasi-exogenous shock, we study changes in the prevalence and composition of mixed-gender teams around 2020.

Our results reveal a significant role for gender in co-teaching practices, with mixed-gender teams occurring consistently less frequently than same-gender teams across institutions and fields. Furthermore, mixed-gender teams arise much less often than expected under a null model that forms teams in a gender-neutral manner while holding field-related and institutional constraints fixed. These findings underscore a persistent and widespread underrepresentation of mixed-gender collaborations in teaching not explained by unobserved differences across fields or institutions. 

Our analysis further reveals a significant and strong association between the gender composition of a teaching team and the course's interdisciplinarity, with mixed-gender teams being more interdisciplinary than all-male teams or courses taught by individual instructors. This evidence suggests that gender diversity can be a potential lever for increasing interdisciplinary teaching in tertiary education. We also find a significant association between team configurations and the fraction of cited female authors. Specifically, classes taught by a female instructor alone tend to cite a higher fraction of female authors than those taught by male instructors, with mixed-gender teams falling in between. This finding highlights teaching practices as an understudied potential driver of women's underrepresentation in academia. Finally, we observe a trend toward novelty: female instructors assign more recent readings than their male counterparts, regardless of team size. This evidence contributes to the debate on the factors shaping the education-innovation gap [@biasi2022education].

# Literature

Research shows that gender and institutional structures shape key aspects of academic work, from team formation to curriculum design. Four strands of this literature are particularly relevant to our study: (a) research on how gender influences collaboration patterns [e.g., @guimera2005team], (b) the representation of women in course materials [e.g., @dion2018gendered], (c) the interdisciplinarity of mixed-gender teams [e.g., @pinheiro2022women], and (d) the propensity of such teams to innovate [e.g., @yang2022gender]. These insights provide the conceptual foundation for our study, which we review in detail below.

## Gender and team formation in academia

<!-- [e.g., @milojevic2014principles; @wuchty2007increasing] -->

Research on scientific collaboration shows that teams play an increasingly important role in knowledge production [e.g., @wuchty2007increasing; @milojevic2014principles]. Moreover, research examining team formation in science and professional networks has found that collaboration patterns exhibit strong gender homophily: individuals tend to associate with others who are similar to them [e.g., @mcpherson2001birds]. In research, scholars in economics, the life sciences, and other fields tend to co-author disproportionately with same-gender colleagues [@mcdowell1992effect; @holman2019researchers; @boschini2007team; @kwiek2021gender]. This form of gender-based sorting is not only driven by institutional constraints, as it appears even in large and diverse research environments [e.g., @kwiek2021gender], it persists across career stages [e.g., @nguyen2025mind], and is considered a contributing factor to gender gaps in outputs [e.g., @ductor2023gender] and inequalities in academia [e.g., @mcdowell1992effect]. Despite this evidence, the collaboration patterns in teaching are less well understood. On the one hand, homophily to influence teaching collaboration as it does in research teams, via individual preferences. On the other, teaching presents unique features. Unlike research collaborations, teaching presents unique features, such as institutionally assigned course responsibilities and the predominantly local, in-person nature of instructional work, that might either constrain or amplify gender homophily. This motivates our effort to understand the prevalence of teaching teams and the gender composition of co-teaching teams across various fields and institutions. 

<!-- 
@love2022impact It also examines gender differences in citation patterns, a driving factor of the persistent gender bias in academia [@boivin2024sexism], including tenure promotion [@weisshaar2017publish], grant success [@bedi2012gender], co-authorships [@boschini2007team], and peer recognition [@sarsons2017recognition;@card2022gender]. While prior research has primarily focused on these areas, we shift our attention to the citation gap in teaching. This issue may not only reinforce existing gender bias in academia but also shape students' learning outcomes and future career choices [@card2022impact], effects that are less well understood. -->

## Gender and women's underrepresentation in academia

A growing body of research examines why women are underrepresented or undercited in academic work. Scholars tend to cite others of the same gender, a kind of homophily [@REFs], and male-dominated fields may confer a “first-mover advantage” to men [@REF]. Other contributing factors include different self-citation patterns [@REFs] and overall gaps in publication output [@REF]. Evidence that these disparities extend into teaching is more limited, but studies indicate that female scholars are underrepresented in course syllabi [@harris2020diversify; @colgan2017gender; @skitka2021gender]. This underrepresentation appears linked to the instructor's gender. For example, in Political Science, instructors' gender predicts the assignment of female-authored readings, suggesting a form of gender homophily in course content [@smith2020gender]. Similarly, in research on political science xxxx, @dion2018gendered finds that female-led teams cite more women, whereas mixed-gender teams do not cite more women than all-male teams. These findings suggest that team composition and dynamics strongly influence decisions about what to cite or include in course readings. A key goal of our study is to investigate these patterns on a large scale across multiple domains and institutions.

## Mixed-Gender Teams and Interdisciplinarity

Recent evidence suggests that women, on average, undertake more interdisciplinary research than men [@pinheiro2022women], and that interdisciplinary collaborations can produce broader scientific outcomes [@specht2022interdisciplinary]. Taken together, this work suggests that the presence of women in a team is associated with more interdisciplinary intellectual contributions. In a teaching context, however, other factors may influence this relationship. For example, being interdisciplinary in resarch does not necessiarly translte into interdisciplinary teaching styles. Furtheormore, students may not demand for interdisciplinary courses. Or there might be institutional constraints where programs are not designed to support interdisciplinarity. Finally, also team dynamics or matching patterns, may prevent instructors to find good partners for interdisciplinary courses. To shed lights on these issues, we explored at a large scale the relationships between course interdisciplinarity, team size, and gender composition in teaching.

## Gender Diversity and Innovation

Extensive research has shown that gender-diverse teams tend to generate more novel and higher-impact ideas than single-gender teams in scientific collaboration [e.g., @yang2022gender]. Related work on research and R&D teams links gender diversity to more radical or innovative outputs, as well as to improved decision-making and performance [@Diaz2013; @apesteguia2012impact; @opstrup2015right; @bear2011role; @campbell2013gender; @love2022impact]. However, in the context of teaching, the relationship between gender diversity and novelty is less well understood. A key challenge lies in defining measures of novelty in a pedagogical setting. Traditional approaches based on journal readings may be insufficient, as syllabi often rely on textbooks, for which scientific novelty is difficult to assess. Moreover, innovation in teaching can take different forms: instructors can adopt new methods that are pedagogically novel but not at the scientific frontier (“teaching novelty”); they can assign more recent materials (“recency”); or they can include papers at the forefront of research (“scientific novelty”). In this study, we examine all three dimensions empirically.

<!-- Another topic we aim to investigate is how gender composition and size of teaching teams is associated with the recenncy and scientific novelty of the readings. Extensive research has shown that gender-diverse teams tend to generate more novel and higher-impact ideas than single-gender teams in scientific collaboration [e.g., @yang2022gender]. Related work on research and R&D teams links gender diversity to more radical or innovative outputs, as well as to improved decision-making and performance [@Diaz2013; @apesteguia2012impact; @opstrup2015right; @bear2011role; @campbell2013gender; @love2022impact]. However, in teaching, the relationship with recency and scientific novelty is less understood. First, it is hard to define novelty in that context, as readings often refer to textbooks instead of journal articles for which we know how to measure scientific novelty. [Referee old text becomeing new insight]. However, we can expect the team composistion to associate with novelty, and we aim to investigate empirically this topic. [Combining teachers cna lead to share practiceis and increase novelty of teaching ...].  
 -->

## Covid-19's impact on academic work 

Our work is also related to research on the impact of COVID-19 on academic work. Research documents that during COVID there was a decrease in research time, project initiation, and scientific productivity, with particularly strong effects for women and researchers with caregiving responsibilities [@myers2020unequal; @viglione2020women; @gao2021potentially;@cui2022gender].^[
    Although the effect of lockdowns and restricted mobility on productivity is found to be country- and field-dependent [@garcia2024silver].
]
At the same time, bibliometric studies show that the crisis reshaped collaboration networks: for instance, a decrease in new co-authorships [@gao2021potentially] and changes in the geographical distribution of collaboration, including an increase in cross-border teaming [@venturini2025effectsremoteworkingscientific]. Commentaries on scientific meetings emphasise that the pandemic-driven shift to virtual conferences reshaped scientific networking: while online formats broadened participation — including a modest increase in women’s representation [@olechnicka2025impact] — they also reduced opportunities for informal, in-person interactions that are seen as vital for collaboration and idea generation [@bray2022together].

In parallel, universities were forced into a rapid transition to online and hybrid teaching, potentially altering how teaching teams are formed and how often instructors collaborate across gender and disciplinary boundaries. We therefore exploit the onset of COVID-19 as a quasi-exogenous shock and estimate an event study around 2020 to examine whether the prevalence and composition of mixed-gender teams changed after the pandemic, conditional on institution and field.

<!-- By focusing on the relationships between teaching and interdisciplinarity, our work also contributes to the literature on the factors influencing interdisciplinarity in academia. There is evidence that interdisciplinarity research is less likely to be funded [@bromham2016interdisciplinary], tends to attract fewer citations when it is highly interdisciplinary [@lariviere2010relationship], and is correlated with the probability of publication in academic journals [@xiang2025evaluating]. Furthermore, students attending colleges with more interdisciplinary courses tend to earn higher earnings after graduation [@evans2016measuring;@han2023interdisciplinary]. We extend this work by examining the association between gender composition and interdisciplinarity in teaching, showing that mixed-gender teams occur less frequently but tend to be more interdisciplinary. This finding suggests that removing barriers to mixed-gender team formation may increase interdisciplinarity in teaching. -->

<!-- We also build on prior research on the underrepresentation of female-authored works in university curricula, which has been shown within specific fields (psychology and international relations) and at a small scale [@skitka2021gender;@colgan2017gender;@harris2020diversify]. Our results reveal a consistent and significant gap in the number of cited works between female and male instructors, which is only partially addressed within mixed-gender co-teaching. This evidence highlights teaching practices as an important factor shaping gender differences in recognition among scholars. -->

<!-- Finally, studies about team formation are especially relevant to our work. These studies have found that mixed-gender teams tend to perform better in various settings [@yang2022gender;@bear2011role]. In research collaboration, for example, mixed-gender teams often obtain more citations, produce more novel research, publish in more prestigious journals, and are more interdisciplinary [@maddi2021gender; @yang2022gender]. Although we do not measure team "performance" as we lack data on students outcomes, our results show that mixed-gender co-teaching teams are consistently less likely to form, but, once created, they tend to deliver different outcomes, especially a higher interdisciplinarity, that previous literature has suggested having an impact on students outcomes as discussed above. -->


<!-- Research on citation behavior also shows that women are cited less
frequently than men in many fields, and that female-authored work tends
to cite women more than male-authored work does (see, e.g., for
international relations @maliniak2013; for astronomy
@caplar2017quantitative; for biomedical sciences @dworkin2020extent; for
economics and finance @wang2021gendered; for mathematics and computer
science @teich2022citation). Notably, @dion2018 find that while
female-authored articles in political science cite more women,
mixed-gender author teams do *not* cite more women than all-male teams. -->
